text.fullText: From: intjsci@everyactioncustom.com To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/3/2026 11:06:07 AM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 ------------------------------------------ [You don't often get email from intjsci@everyactioncustom.com. Learn why this is important at https://aka.ms/LearnAboutSenderIdentification ] Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Dear Administrator Lee Zeldin, I'm a retired U.S. EPS career employee who witnessed first hand serious water quality issues that harmed people's health and their recreational opportunities and caused significant financial burden to local governments and other entities. I've written about water quality and quantity issues quite often and can say with 100% certainty that water quality and quantity are inseparable. If you degrade water quality enough, that water actually becomes polluted water, as is obvious, and polluted water is more closely related to cesspool contamination than it is to what we have almost always understood to be water. Polluted water, as just one example, "killed" Lake Erie and Lake Erie's commercial fishing and recreational boating and angling industries. While Lake Erie has recovered some of its former commercial fishing and boating and angling industries, still extremely expensive water quality issues have harmed drinking water quality, rendering Lake Erie's water unsuitable for drinking water use in Toledo, Ohio, as one recent example. The new water quality issues under today's regulatory requirements differ from those of the past because these are new, such as the advent of "harmful algae blooms," invasive species and other threats superimposed upon just about all previous threats. So, rather than allow myself to go on about the issues of degraded waters, I conducted a quick AI search, which is provided below and covers the most current major issues, yet does not capture the true gravity of what is at stake for Americans and the reputation and dignity of America itself. "What is the danger of releasing clean water protections federally? "AI Overview Redefining "Waters of the United States" (WOTUS) to exclude wetlands and small streams—a move often spurred by Supreme Court rulings like Sackett v. EPA—removes federal protection for millions of acres of aquatic ecosystems. This deregulation risks significant increases in water pollution, heightened flood risks, destruction of wildlife habitats, and decreased drinking water quality. Key Dangers of Reclassifying Wetlands and Small Streams: Increased Flood Risks and Reduced Natural Protection: Wetlands act as natural sponges, absorbing floodwaters and storm surges. Removing protection allows for development in these areas, making surrounding communities more vulnerable to flooding. Widespread Water Pollution: Small, ephemeral streams and wetlands filter pollutants before they reach larger rivers and lakes. Without federal oversight, these areas can be filled, paved over, or used for waste disposal, increasing pollution in connected, larger water bodies. Loss of Biodiversity: Wetlands provide crucial habitats for wildlife, including endangered species. The removal of protections can accelerate the destruction of these habitats and threaten the survival of various fish and species. Reduced Drinking Water Protection: Many municipalities rely on small streams and headwaters that feed into larger water sources. Removing safeguards for these smaller, upstream waters directly impacts the quality of downstream drinking water sources. Regulatory Inconsistency and Increased Burden on States: While intended to reduce federal overreach on private landowners, this change shifts the responsibility to states, many of which may lack the resources or regulatory frameworks to protect these water bodies effectively." Thank you for all of the incredibly important work that you do at the U.S. EPA. All of the best to you and your agency. Thomas J. Slabe Sincerely, Thomas Dewittville, NY
